summary: Gruber argues forcefully that Safari's refusal to display favicons in browser tabs is a significant usability failure and likely the single biggest reason Mac users choose Chrome over Safari. Following overwhelming reader feedback to a previous Safari vs. Chrome comparison, he presents screenshot evidence showing that Safari tabs become literally unidentifiable with a dozen or more tabs open, while Chrome's favicon-equipped tabs remain usable. He speculates Apple's resistance stems from a desire to maintain a monochromatic toolbar aesthetic, but argues this trades a large usability cost for a debatable aesthetic gain. He further contends that Safari's favicon omission hurts not just Safari but the entire Mac platform, since users stuck on Chrome miss out on Safari's superior battery life.
key_points: Safari tabs become completely unidentifiable beyond a dozen tabs because they show no favicons and truncate titles to nothing, while Chrome tabs remain usable through recognizable icons
The Mac platform has historically prioritized visual iconography over text labels since 1984, making Safari's text-only tabs philosophically inconsistent with Mac design principles
The feedback came not just from developers but from designers too, undermining the aesthetic argument for text-only tabs
Safari's favicon omission has downstream platform effects: users stuck on Chrome get 1-2 hours less battery life on MacBooks
Apple's refusal to offer favicons even as a preference setting seems deliberately obstinate given it may be Safari's most-requested feature
money_quotes: There are a huge number of Daring Fireball readers who use Chrome because it shows favicons on tabs and would switch to Safari if it did.
The balance between what looks best and what works best is way out of whack with Safari's tabs.
The argument that Safari's tab bar should remain text-only is like arguing that MacOS should change its Command-Tab switcher and Dock from showing icons to showing only the names of applications.
The Mac has been famous ever since 1984 for placing more visual significance on icons than on names. The Mac attracts visual thinkers and its design encourages visual thinking.
I think Safari's lack of favicons in tabs, combined with its corresponding crumminess when displaying a dozen or more tabs in a window, is the single biggest reason why so many Mac users use Chrome.
The fact that it's not even a preference, even though it may well be the single most-common feature request for Safari, seems downright spiteful.
And not just mean-to-others spiteful, but cut-off-your-nose-to-spite-your-face spiteful.
key_insight: Safari's stubborn refusal to show favicons in tabs sacrifices substantial usability for a debatable aesthetic preference, driving users to Chrome and costing them battery life in the process.
